title: Manage Customer Keys with Azure Key Vault

sections.heading: About Customer-Managed Keys with Azure Key Vault
sections.body:
Customer key management in Atlas follows a process called envelope encryption.
This process creates multiple layers of encryption by encrypting one key with another
key. To enable customer key management, Atlas uses the following encryption keys:

sections.heading: Example
sections.body:
Consider the following encryption hierarchy for a three-node replica set.
Atlas uses the CMK (customer-managed key) from Google Cloud KMS to encrypt a unique
MongoDB Master Key for each node in the cluster. Each node also contains
three databases, each of which is encrypted with a unique
per-database encryption key. When the cluster starts up,
Atlas decrypts the MongoDB Master Key by using the CMK (customer-managed key) from
Google Cloud KMS and supplies this to the MongoDB Server.

> **Note:** If you revoke Atlas's access to the CMK (customer-managed key), Atlas shuts down the
> nodes in your cluster and you can't access your data until
> you restore access to the CMK (customer-managed key).

![Diagram of CMK workflow with AKV and Atlas](/images/azure-kms-cmk-diagram.png)

sections.heading: What You Need to Know
sections.body:
After configuring Atlas to use your AKV (Azure Key Vault) CMK (customer-managed key), learn more
[About Rotating Your Azure Key Identifier](azure-kms-rotate-key) and [About Azure Key Vault Failover During an Outage](akv-failover).

sections.heading: About Rotating Your Azure Key Identifier
sections.body:
> **Note:** This feature is not available for any of the following deployments:
>
> - Free clusters
>
> - Flex clusters
>
> To learn more, see [Limits](limits).

sections.heading: MongoDB Master Key - MongoDB Responsibility
sections.body:
When you use your own cloud provider KMS (Key Management Service), Atlas automatically rotates
MongoDB Master Keys at least every 90 days. Your key rotation will begin
during a [Configure Maintenance Window](configure-maintenance-window),
if you have one configured. Deferring maintenance (either manually or automatically)
may cause the key to be rotated past the 90-day mark.
Keys are rotated on a
rolling basis and the process does not require the data to be rewritten.

sections.heading: Rotate your Azure Key ID - Your Responsibility
sections.body:
Atlas does *not* automatically rotate the Key Identifier used for
Azure Key Vault.

Atlas automatically creates an [encryption key rotation alert](<Azure encryption key elapsed time since last rotation is above (n) days>) to remind you to rotate your Azure Key Identifier every 90 days by
default when you [Manage Customer Keys with Azure Key Vault](security-azure-kms)
for an Atlas project.

You can [rotate](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/container-registry/tutorial-rotate-revoke-customer-managed-keys)
CMK (customer-managed key) stored in Azure Key Vault yourself or configure
your Azure Key Vault to automatically rotate your keys. If you
configure [automatic rotation](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/virtual-machines/disk-encryption#automatic-key-rotation-of-customer-managed-keys)
in Azure Key Vault, the default time period for rotation is
approximately 365 days.

If you have already set up an automatic rotation in Azure Key Vault and
don't want to receive the Atlas alert to rotate your Azure Key
Identifier every 90 days, you can [Configure Alert Settings](configure-alerts) the
default alert period to be greater than 365 days.

sections.heading: About Azure Key Vault Failover During an Outage
sections.body:
During a regional outage, your AKV (Azure Key Vault) region might become unavailable. If
this happens, Azure (Microsoft Azure) automatically routes incoming Key Vault requests
to a pre-assigned secondary region. To learn more, see [Azure Key
Vault Failover](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/key-vault/general/disaster-recovery-guidance#failover)
and [Regional Pairings](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/reliability/cross-region-replication-azure#azure-cross-region-replication-pairings-for-all-geographies).

If both regions are down, you can manually migrate your key to a
region outside of the regional pairing. To learn more, see
[Move a Key Vault across Regions](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/key-vault/general/move-region).

> **Note:** If you've enabled [Encryption at Rest using Customer Key Management](security-kms-encryption), you can perform
> encrypt and decrypt operations while at least one node is still
> available during the outage. Atlas won't shut down your
> clusters.

For certain regions, Azure (Microsoft Azure) doesn't support automatic failover. To
learn more, see [Azure documentation](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/key-vault/general/disaster-recovery-guidance).

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
To configure customer key management, you must have [Project Owner](<Project Owner>)
access to the project.

Users with [Organization Owner](<Organization Owner>) access must add themselves to the
project as a `Project Owner`.

You must grant Atlas access to your AKV (Azure Key Vault) using a
**Permission Model**. You can grant access by using a Key Vault
Access Policy or Azure (Microsoft Azure) RBAC (Role Based Access Control).

Vault Access Policy

Azure RBAC

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
If you use access policies, do the following in the Azure (Microsoft Azure) UI to
grant Atlas access to your AKV (Azure Key Vault).

- Under **Access Control (IAM)** settings, grant the
  `Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/read` permission to your application.

- In the **Access Policies** page, create an access
  policy with the following **Key Permissions**:

  | Grant this permission... | To...                                                                                                          |
  | ------------------------ | -------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  | Get                      | Read the metadata about the key stored in your AKV (Azure Key Vault).                                          |
  | Encrypt                  | Use the keys in your AKV (Azure Key Vault) to encrypt data.                                                    |
  | Decrypt                  | Use the keys in your AKV (Azure Key Vault) to decrypt data that has been previously encrypted with those keys. |

- Add the same app as the principal and configure the access policy.

sections.heading: Required Access
sections.body:
If your AKV (Azure Key Vault) uses RBAC (Role Based Access Control), you
must do the following in the Azure (Microsoft Azure) UI **Access Control
(IAM)** settings to grant Atlas access to your AKV (Azure Key Vault).

- Grant the following `actions` permissions to the application.

  | Grant this permission...            | To...                                                                 |
  | ----------------------------------- | --------------------------------------------------------------------- |
  | Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/read      | Read the properties of a Key Vault                                    |
  | Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/keys/read | Read the metadata about the key stored in your AKV (Azure Key Vault). |

  ```json
  {
    "permissions": [
       {
         "actions": [
           "Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/read"
           "Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/keys/read"
         ]
       }
       ...
    ]
  }
  ```

- Grant the following `dataActions` permissions to the application.

  | Grant this permission...                      | To...                                                                                            |
  | --------------------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------------ |
  | Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/keys/read           | Read the metadata about the key stored in your AKV (Azure Key Vault).                            |
  | Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/keys/encrypt/action | Use the keys in your AKV (Azure Key Vault) to encrypt data.                                      |
  | Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/keys/decrypt/action | Use the keys in your AKV (Azure Key Vault) to decrypt data previously encrypted with those keys. |

  ```json
  {
    "permissions": [
       ...
       {
         "dataActions": [
           "Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/keys/read",
           "Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/keys/encrypt/action",
           "Microsoft.KeyVault/vaults/keys/decrypt/action"
         ]
       }
    ]
  }
  ```

sections.heading: Next Steps
sections.body:
You can use a customer-managed key (CMK) from Azure Key Vault (AKV) over
a public network or over Azure (Microsoft Azure) Private Endpoints. To learn more, see
the following:

- [Manage Customer Keys with Azure Key Vault Over Private Endpoints](security-azure-kms-pvt-endpoint)

- [Manage Customer Keys with Azure Key Vault Over a Public Network](security-azure-kms-public-network)

> **Note:** If you've enabled [Encryption at Rest using Customer Key Management](security-kms-encryption), you can
> perform encrypt and decrypt operations while at least one node
> is still available during the outage.

sections.heading: Enable Customer-Managed Keys for Search Nodes
sections.body:
By default, MongoDB and [search processes](about-mongot) run on the same nodes.
With this architecture, customer-managed encryption applies to your database data,
but it does not apply to search indexes.

When you enable [Search Nodes](what-is-search-node), search processes run on separate nodes. This allows
you to enable [Enable Customer Key Management for Search Nodes](enable-search-node-encryption),
so you can encrypt both database data and search indexes with the same
customer-managed keys for comprehensive encryption coverage.

> **Note:** Database nodes and Search Nodes use different encryption methods
> with the same customer-managed keys. Database nodes use the
> [WiredTiger Encrypted Storage Engine](manual/core/security-encryption-at-rest),
> while Search Nodes use encryption at the disk level.

> **Important:** 
>
> This feature is available across KMS (Key Management Service) providers, but the
> Search Nodes must be on AWS (Amazon Web Services).
